claim: Answer leveling (structured brainstorming with increasing depth levels, typically level 3-4 preferred) reliably produces more creative and specific ideas than unstructured brainstorming.
actionable: Use answer leveling prompts when brainstorming; ask for level 3 or 4 depth specifically; structure requests to build from general ideas to specific implementations.
source_title: 101 Ways To Use AI In Your Daily Life

claim: Voice mode for AI assistants (thinking out loud with voice) unlocks different cognitive processes than typing; ideal for brainstorming, negotiation practice, and problem-solving.
actionable: Use voice input/output when thinking through complex problems; switch from text mode for brainstorming sessions; record and review voice-based work sessions.
source_title: 101 Ways To Use AI In Your Daily Life

claim: Vision model real-time streaming allows step-by-step task guidance; share your screen and get AI to narrate/direct you through unfamiliar interfaces or processes.
actionable: For learning new software or troubleshooting visual tasks, use Google AI Studio real-time streaming; share your screen to get live step-by-step guidance.
source_title: 101 Ways To Use AI In Your Daily Life

claim: Vibe coding tools (no-code/low-code AI builders) enable non-technical people to build functional apps without writing code; Lovable, Bolt, and Cursor are entry-friendly options.
actionable: For non-technical users wanting to build apps, start with Lovable or Bolt; for those with some coding background, progress to Cursor or Windsurf.
source_title: 101 Ways To Use AI In Your Daily Life

claim: Building personal AI agents for automation (lead generation, customer service, database querying) is feasible with no-code frameworks; N8N for no-code, OpenAI SDK for code.
actionable: For small automations, use N8N (no-code); for custom logic, use OpenAI Agents SDK; start with simple use cases before scaling to complex workflows.
source_title: 101 Ways To Use AI In Your Daily Life
